title: Associations of longitudinal BMI percentile classification patterns in early childhood with neighborhood-level social determinants of health.
ehr_evidence: We extracted EHR data from 2012-2019 for a children’s health system that includes 2 hospitals and wide network of outpatient clinics spanning 5 East Coast states in the US.
summary: The study identifies distinct longitudinal BMI-percentile classification groups in children aged 0-7 years and examines their associations with 27 neighborhood-level social determinants of health (SDOH) using EHR data.
key_findings: Five BMI-percentile classification groups emerged: always obesity, overweight most of the time, increasing BMI-percentile, decreasing BMI-percentile, and always normal weight.
Children in the obesity/overweight clusters were more likely to live in neighborhoods with higher poverty, unemployment, crowded households, single-parent households, and lower preschool enrollment.
study_design: Retrospective cohort study using data-driven and algorithmic clustering and multinomial logistic regression
population: 36,910 children aged 0-7 years in a children's health system across 5 East Coast states in the US
